heb.paragraph: "Hear me, LORD, my plea is just (ṣedeq)" (v.1). David opens with a claim of righteousness — not sinlessness but innocence in this specific case. He invites God’s examination: "test me and you will find nothing" (v.3).

panels.ctx: David asks God to hear his righteous cause (v.1), examine his heart by night (v.3), and protect him like the apple of God’s eye (v.8). He has kept his feet from wrong paths (v.5). The psalm is a prayer for vindication against specific enemies.

cross.note: "Vindicate me, LORD... test me" — the same examination-invitation.
"He guarded him as the apple of his eye" — the same intimate protection image.

notes.note: "Hear my righteous plea. Test me, LORD, and try me; examine my heart — you will find nothing." David invites divine scrutiny. This is bold: "search me and find nothing wrong."
"I call on you, my God, for you will answer me. Show me the wonders of your great love." Confidence that God both hears and responds.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "ṣedeq" opens the psalm — David’s cause IS righteousness. The prayer claims justice, not mercy.
Calvin: the invitation to be tested is not arrogance but integrity. Calvin: the righteous person does not fear divine examination.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "you have examined my heart and visited me by night" — the nighttime visit is when defences are down. God tests David when he is most vulnerable.
Kidner: the invitation to be examined echoes Psalm 139:23. David does not fear scrutiny because he knows his own integrity in this matter.
VanGemeren: the psalm combines lament with innocence-assertion. David does not merely ask for help; he makes a legal case.
Goldingay: "you will find nothing" is absolute confidence in a specific claim, not in overall sinlessness. Context matters.

sections.header: Verses 8–15 — “Keep Me as the Apple of Your Eye”

heb.gloss: apple of the eye / pupil (lit. "little man of the daughter [of the eye]")
heb.paragraph: "Keep me as the apple of your eye (ʾishōn bat); hide me in the shadow of your wings" (v.8). The "apple of the eye" is the pupil — the most reflexively protected part of the body. David asks to be guarded as instinctively as the eye guards its centre. The "shadow of wings" adds the mother-bird image.

panels.ctx: David asks for the most intimate protection: apple of the eye, shadow of wings (v.8). The enemies are described as fat, arrogant, lion-like (vv.10–12). David asks God to confront them (v.13). The psalm’s closing contrast: the wicked have their reward in this life; David will be satisfied "with seeing your face when I awake" (v.15) — another hint of resurrection.

cross.note: "Whoever touches you touches the apple of his eye" — the same intimate protection language applied to Israel.
"Under whose wings you have come to take refuge" — the same wing-shelter image.
"When Christ appears, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is" — seeing God’s face on waking.

notes.note: "Keep me as the apple of your eye; hide me in the shadow of your wings." Double protection imagery: the most instinctive (eye-guarding) and the most tender (wing-sheltering).

"People of this world whose reward is in this life... as for me, I will be vindicated and will see your face; when I awake, I will be satisfied with seeing your likeness." The ultimate contrast: they have their reward now; David’s reward is seeing God’s face after death.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "apple of the eye" literally "ʾishōn-bat-ʿayin" — "little man, daughter of the eye." The pupil reflects a tiny image of whoever looks into it. David asks to be reflected in God’s eye.

Calvin: "when I awake" is the resurrection morning. Calvin: the contrast with v.14 is eschatological: the wicked have their belly-full in this life; the righteous will be satisfied only when they see God face to face.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "apple of the eye" is idiomatic for the most precious, most protected thing. The pupil is automatically shielded by the eyelid at any threat.
The NET: "when I awake" (ḥāqîṣ) can mean waking from sleep or waking from death. The context (contrasted with the wicked’s this-life reward) favours the latter.
Kidner: "when I awake" suggests resurrection: the righteous person wakes to God’s presence. The wicked had their portion in this life; the righteous have theirs in the next.
VanGemeren: "seeing your face" when awakened connects to Psalm 16’s resurrection hope. The Psalter’s theology of the afterlife develops through these early psalms.
Goldingay: "satisfied with seeing your likeness" — the satisfaction the wicked found in wealth (v.14), the righteous find in God’s face. Different currencies of fulfilment.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThough people tried to bribe me, I have kept myself from the ways of the violent through what your lips have commanded.ESVWith regard to the works of man, by the word of your lips I have avoided the ways of the violent.NIVfrom the wicked who are out to destroy me, from my mortal enemies who surround me.ESVfrom the wicked who do me violence, my deadly enemies who surround me.

rec.text: ""Hear my righteous plea. Test me, LORD, and try me; examine my heart — you will find nothing." David invites divine scrutiny. This is bold: "search me and find nothing wrong.""

rows.label: The Examination — 17:1–7
rows.text: "Test me and find nothing." A righteous cause, a bold invitation.

hebtext.note: "Hear me, LORD, my plea is just (ṣedeq)" (v.1). David opens with a claim of righteousness — not sinlessness but innocence in this specific case. He invites God’s examination: "test me and you will find nothing" (v.3).

hebtext.gloss: apple of the eye / pupil (lit. "little man of the daughter [of the eye]")
hebtext.note: "Keep me as the apple of your eye (ʾishōn bat); hide me in the shadow of your wings" (v.8). The "apple of the eye" is the pupil — the most reflexively protected part of the body. David asks to be guarded as instinctively as the eye guards its centre. The "shadow of wings" adds the mother-bird image.

thread.text: "Vindicate me, LORD... test me" — the same examination-invitation.
"He guarded him as the apple of his eye" — the same intimate protection image.
"Whoever touches you touches the apple of his eye" — the same intimate protection language applied to Israel.
"Under whose wings you have come to take refuge" — the same wing-shelter image.
"When Christ appears, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is" — seeing God’s face on waking.

themes.note: Prophecy: "when I awake" hints at resurrection. Faith: inviting divine examination. Mercy: apple-of-eye and wing-shadow protection.
